network.overall_strategy: This network targets threonine primarily through literature-supported low-temperature surface cyanosulfidic chemistry, with hydrothermal environments supplying reduced carbon feedstocks and concentration mechanisms rather than direct high-temperature threonine synthesis. The densest validated core uses HCN-centered photoredox chemistry to generate acetaldehyde, lactaldehyde, and glyceraldehyde, then converges on threonine aminonitrile and its hydrolysis product, a threonine/allothreonine racemic pool. Additional pathways represent experimentally observed source regimes (spark discharge), a plausible hydantoin sink route, and lower-confidence but literature-discussed background routes (HCN polymer hydrolysate mixtures, ammonia-aldehyde hydroxy-amino-acid families, reverse aldol relation, and α-keto-acid reductive amination extrapolation). Because abiotic synthesis yields threonine/allothreonine and D/L mixtures, the network treats threonine as arising from that mixed pool and then being rapidly captured in biochemical peptide formation to mitigate instability. Hydrothermal steps are kept to moderate vent conditions and connected causally into surface chemistry via formate/formaldehyde/glycolaldehyde feedstocks and concentration.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent-like pH 9-11; moderate Lost City-type conditions; concentration in micropores

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-catalyzed reduction of CO2 by H2 on iron-sulfur surfaces yields formate as a vent-exportable reduced carbon intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Formate generation on FeS/Fe3S4 under alkaline vent conditions is consistent with iron-sulfur world and vent geochemistry, and such vents can concentrate organics ~5000-fold in micropores while preserving products better than hotter black-smoker settings (White et al., 2015; Lane and Martin, 2012). This reaction is included as the hydrothermal carbon-entry node feeding lower-temperature surface chemistry; it avoids direct high-temperature threonine formation, which is disfavored by threonine instability (Truong et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Surface photoredox conversion of HCN to formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: UV-illuminated Cu(I) cyanide photoredox system supported in phosphate-buffered pool; TiO2 included as photocatalytic mineral surface
conditions.other: UV exposure; H2S reductant implied by cyanosulfidic setting; evaporitic or volcanic pool

reactions.mechanism: HCN in a cyanosulfidic photoredox network undergoes reductive homologation to simple carbonyl compounds, generating formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde as a shared aldehyde pool.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson and Sutherland showed UV irradiation of Cu(I) cyanide complexes with H2S as reductant converts HCN into formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and acetaldehyde; Patel et al. placed this in a broader cyanosulfidic protometabolism tied to nucleotide precursor synthesis (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015). This reaction intentionally uses an HCN-centered entry rather than unsupported formate-to-HCN chemistry, addressing prior alignment problems. Hydrothermal formate is included as a transferred reduced-carbon feedstock and concentration aid rather than as the sole mechanistic carbon source.

reactions.name: Aldol homologation of formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: wet pool chemistry; sugar-stabilizing borate-rich setting

reactions.mechanism: Aldol-type sugar homologation generates the triose glyceraldehyde from lower aldehydes, with borate helping stabilize reactive sugar products.
reactions.reasoning: Glyceraldehyde is a key branch point in Sutherland-type networks and directly enters threonine aminonitrile formation; borate minerals are widely invoked to stabilize such sugars under prebiotic surface conditions (Patel et al., 2015). This step is a connecting necessity consistent with the research emphasis that glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde bridge RNA and amino-acid chemistry.

reactions.name: Photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: UV-illuminated Cu(I) cyanide photoredox chemistry with H2S reductant on mineral surfaces
conditions.other: 2 h irradiation; cyanosulfidic conditions; lactaldehyde reported at 19% yield from acetaldehyde cyanohydrin reduction

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde first forms a cyanohydrin with HCN, then photoreductive processing in the Cu-cyanide/H2S UV network yields lactaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: This is the best-characterized threonine-specific entry point: Ritson and Sutherland experimentally showed photoreduction of acetaldehyde cyanohydrin to lactaldehyde at 19% yield, directly enabling the Strecker route to threonine (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013). Mineral photocatalytic surfaces are specified to satisfy network constraints while preserving the literature-specific Cu(I) cyanide photoredox context.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from lactaldehyde

conditions.other: classic Strecker sequence via imine formation; anoxic; racemic/diastereomeric outcome expected

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine, followed by cyanide addition to give 2-amino-3-hydroxybutyronitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The lactaldehyde Strecker sequence is explicitly described in the deep research as the most complete threonine route and gives the threonine aminonitrile precursor to threonine/allothreonine (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015). Product stereochemistry is expected to be nonselective, yielding threonine and allothreonine after hydrolysis.

reactions.name: Direct Strecker synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from glyceraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: borate-stabilized sugar pool with phosphate-buffered aqueous phase

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde undergoes Strecker addition with ammonia and cyanide to generate the same threonine aminonitrile hub via an alternative aldehyde precursor.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. identified a parallel route in which glyceraldehyde rather than lactaldehyde furnishes the threonine aminonitrile, linking amino-acid and ribonucleotide precursor chemistry through a shared branch point (Patel et al., 2015). This provides one of the principal convergence routes in the network.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine/allothreonine pool

conditions.other: mild aqueous hydrolysis; rapid use favored because of threonine instability

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydrolysis converts the aminonitrile to the corresponding amino acid pool, producing racemic threonine and allothreonine diastereomers.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of 2-amino-3-hydroxybutyronitrile to threonine/allothreonine is the terminal step of the Strecker route described for lactaldehyde and glyceraldehyde precursors (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015). The mixed stereochemical outcome is explicitly retained to avoid overclaiming selective threonine formation. Fast downstream capture is justified by threonine fragility (Truong et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin formation from lactaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: ammonium carbonate-rich evaporitic setting; mineral concentration surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde reacts with cyanide and ammonia/carbonate equivalents to form a hydroxyethyl hydantoin intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Pulletikurti et al. established Bucherer-Bergs logic as a prebiotic amino-acid route and emphasized hydantoin intermediates as thermodynamic sinks; the deep research specifically proposes the threonine analog 5-(1-hydroxyethyl)hydantoin from lactaldehyde (Pulletikurti et al., 2022). This is included as a literature-guided alternative to direct Strecker hydrolysis.

reactions.name: Hydantoin hydrolysis to threonine/allothreonine pool

conditions.other: prolonged hydrolysis in aqueous pool; mixed stereochemical output expected

reactions.mechanism: Ring opening and hydrolysis of the hydantoin intermediate release the corresponding amino-acid pool.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin hydrolysis to amino acids is the defining completion step of the Bucherer-Bergs route; while the threonine-specific case is an extrapolation from established analogous chemistry, it is directly identified in the research report as the plausible threonine hydantoin path (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; Lerner and Cooper, 2005). This step is therefore included but should be regarded as less directly demonstrated than the canonical Strecker route.

reactions.name: Electric discharge generation of HCN and aldehyde pool from simple gases

conditions.temperature: transient plasma with aqueous collection near ambient
conditions.catalyst: spark discharge over reduced gas mixtures with H2S-enhanced chemistry
conditions.other: represents CH4/N2/H2O/NH3 and CH4/NH3/CO2/H2S discharge experiments; products collected as complex mixtures

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge in reduced gas mixtures generates cyanide and aldehydes in situ, including precursors feeding lactaldehyde and threonine-forming Strecker chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Spark-discharge experiments confirmed threonine formation from simple gases and imply in situ generation of HCN, NH3, and aldehydes including lactaldehyde; H2S-containing setups were particularly productive for hydroxylated amino acids (Ring et al., 1972; Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011). This reaction is deliberately framed as a source-term mixture generator rather than a one-step clean threonine synthesis, addressing prior overcompression concerns.

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN-rich polymer precursor pool

conditions.catalyst: UV exposure and concentration on mineral surfaces

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes under concentrated, irradiated conditions to nitrile-rich oligomer pools represented here by DAMN as a tractable node.
reactions.reasoning: HCN oligomerization to DAMN is established, and broader HCN polymer hydrolysates are known to yield mixtures containing threonine among other amino acids, though with low selectivity (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013). This node is included as a background source branch and not treated as a clean target-directed path.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis and rearrangement of HCN oligomer pool to amino-acid mixture including threonine/allothreonine

conditions.other: heterogeneous hydrolysis of complex nitrile polymer residue; low selectivity

reactions.mechanism: Complex oligomer hydrolysis releases a mixed amino-acid distribution in which threonine/allothreonine can appear as minor products.
reactions.reasoning: Review of 24 experimental HCN-polymer conditions found that hydrolysates can contain threonine among broader amino-acid mixtures, but the route is diffuse and nonselective (Ruiz-Bermejo et al., 2013). This step is therefore explicitly low-confidence and mixture-forming, included only as a background auxiliary route rather than a primary synthesis path.

reactions.name: Ammonia-aldehyde aqueous chemistry producing hydroxy amino-acid isomer pool

conditions.other: family-generating, low-selectivity process; distribution of C3/C4 hydroxy amino acid structural isomers

reactions.mechanism: Mixed aldehydes react with ammonia in aqueous solution to generate families of hydroxy amino-acid isomers, of which threonine/allothreonine are possible members.
reactions.reasoning: Koga and Naraoka demonstrated that labeled aldehydes plus ammonia produce hydroxy amino-acid families including C3 and C4 structural isomers matching meteoritic distributions, but not a clean selective threonine route (Koga and Naraoka, 2022). This node is intentionally framed as a nonspecific source of threonine-related products rather than a selective target-forming reaction.

reactions.name: Conceptual reverse-aldol condensation of glycine and acetaldehyde to threonine/allothreonine pool

conditions.other: high glycine concentration or catalytic assistance would be required; thermodynamically unfavorable

reactions.mechanism: Reverse of threonine aldol cleavage conceptually joins glycine and acetaldehyde to a β-hydroxy amino acid framework.
reactions.reasoning: The glycine + acetaldehyde relation to threonine is structurally valid, but Fesko notes the reverse condensation is thermodynamically unfavorable and no purely prebiotic demonstration exists (Fesko, 2016). This route is therefore explicitly speculative and low-confidence, included only to represent a literature-discussed conceptual connection rather than a validated synthesis.

reactions.name: Speculative formation of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate from glycolaldehyde/acetaldehyde-derived carbonyl network

conditions.other: network-bridging extrapolation; not directly demonstrated for this substrate

reactions.mechanism: Carbonyl homologation/oxidation within a mixed aldehyde pool is hypothesized to access the α-keto-β-hydroxy acid precursor of threonine.
reactions.reasoning: The deep research identifies 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate as the keto-acid precursor to threonine but does not provide a demonstrated prebiotic synthesis of it. This node is therefore marked speculative and used only as a minimal connector for the literature-discussed reductive amination principle (Muchowska et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Reductive amination of 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate to threonine/allothreonine pool

conditions.catalyst: Fe2+ or Ni-mediated reductive amination principle
conditions.other: pH ~8 for Ni/H2 system; threonine-specific substrate not directly tested

reactions.mechanism: Reductive amination converts the α-keto acid precursor into the corresponding amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of α-keto acids to amino acids is established prebiotically with Fe2+ at 70°C and with H2/Ni at 22°C, but the threonine-specific keto acid precursor was not directly tested; thus this step is a principled extrapolation rather than a demonstrated threonine synthesis (Muchowska et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2024). It is kept in the Biochemical environment to avoid unsupported harsh hydrothermal threonine formation.

reactions.name: Peptide capture of unstable threonine/allothreonine pool with glycine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling in clay-rich pool; rapid capture mitigates threonine decomposition

reactions.mechanism: Dehydrative peptide bond formation captures labile threonine/allothreonine into a dipeptide pool under cycling conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Wet-dry cycling and clay-rich interfaces are established to promote prebiotic peptide formation, and rapid incorporation of threonine into oligomers is a chemically sensible response to its documented instability (Lahav et al., 1978; Kitadai et al., 2015; Damer and Deamer, 2020; Truong et al., 2019). The target is therefore defined as a captured threonine-containing dipeptide pool rather than isolated free L-threonine, avoiding unsupported stereochemical selection.

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic lactaldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: HCN photoredox chemistry generates acetaldehyde, which is converted to lactaldehyde and then to threonine aminonitrile followed by hydrolysis to the threonine/allothreonine pool.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic glyceraldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: HCN photoredox chemistry generates glycolaldehyde and formaldehyde, which homologate to glyceraldehyde that directly furnishes threonine aminonitrile and then threonine/allothreonine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface lactaldehyde route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction supplies reduced carbon and concentration context feeding the surface cyanosulfidic aldehyde network, then the validated lactaldehyde Strecker route produces threonine precursors.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface glyceraldehyde route
pathways.description: Vent-derived reduced carbon feeds a surface aldehyde pool that converges through glyceraldehyde into threonine aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin route from lactaldehyde
pathways.description: Lactaldehyde formed in cyanosulfidic chemistry enters a hydantoin sink pathway before hydrolysis to the threonine/allothreonine pool.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge fed lactaldehyde Strecker route
pathways.description: Electric discharge chemistry supplies HCN and aldehydes including acetaldehyde/lactaldehyde precursors, which then enter the validated Strecker sequence.

pathways.name: Spark-discharge fed hydantoin route
pathways.description: Discharge-generated aldehydes and cyanide feed the Bucherer-Bergs branch through hydroxyethyl hydantoin.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysate background route
pathways.description: HCN oligomerization gives a DAMN-rich polymeric precursor pool whose hydrolysis can release minor amounts of threonine/allothreonine in complex mixtures.

pathways.name: Ammonia-aldehyde hydroxy amino acid family route
pathways.description: Mixed aldehydes with ammonia generate a family of hydroxy amino-acid isomers including possible threonine/allothreonine members.

pathways.name: Speculative α-keto acid reductive amination route
pathways.description: A carbonyl network is hypothesized to access 2-oxo-3-hydroxybutyrate, which then undergoes literature-grounded reductive amination in principle to give threonine/allothreonine.
